narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your background, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50, which stands above the typical 3.21 average for students in our dataset. Your family comes from a middle-income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, which aligns with the vast majority of applicants who choose that path. However, the admissions model, trained on data from countless students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 42.7% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try—high enough to tip the decision toward rejecting your application, as the school prioritizes those likely to pass and succeed on that critical exam. The biggest factor pulling the prediction away from failure was your strong undergraduate GPA, which significantly boosted the model's confidence in your potential to pass. On the flip side, your LSAT score of 24—well below the average of 36.7—had the strongest opposing pull, strongly signaling to the model a higher risk of bar exam struggles compared to typical successful students. Your Year 1 school decile of 5, slightly under the average of 5.71, offered some positive support toward passing, while your lower Year 3 decile of 3 nudged it slightly toward failure risk. Your middle family income and full-time status provided smaller but helpful offsets in the model's eyes. Overall, while your GPA and early academic standing shine brighter than many, the LSAT gap loomed largest, placing your profile among those the model sees as riskier for first-time bar passage than the stronger, higher-scoring averages. This isn't a reflection of your worth or potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law—many overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your Year 3 performance if possible, and reapplying with those updates, as even small improvements in key areas can shift the model's view dramatically. You're capable of turning this around, and we're rooting for your success. Keep pushing.
